summary:
Radiation therapy kills cancer cells primarily by inducing double-stranded DNA breaks, triggering mitotic catastrophe due to defective DNA repair pathways common in malignancies. This leads directly to cellular apoptosis or necrosis through irreversible genomic instability.

The correct answer choice reflects this core pathophysiological principle.

Okay, I need to summarize Agent1's reasoning based strictly on the requirements provided — only two sentences focusing solely on key clinical points about how radiation affects cancer cells.

First sentence should capture the main molecular event: radiation causing double-stranded DNA breaks. Second sentence must explain why these cause cell death – specifically mentioning mitotic catastrophe from poor DNA repair in cancer cells.

I'll avoid any mention of irrelevant concepts like thymidine dimers or other unrelated processes since those aren't part of the actual radiological pathway being discussed here.

Final summary will be concise but clinically precise.
